topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box was tucked deep in the attic, smelling faintly of old paper and lavender. Maya had been cleaning out the spare room when she found it—a sturdy cardboard container overflowing with meticulously organized folders. Each one contained a completed assignment, from ancient history essays to complex calculus proofs, dating back to elementary school. Her breath hitched in her throat. She pulled out a brightly colored worksheet from the top. The neat, looping handwriting of her mother filled her vision. A strange, cold pressure settled in her chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. She dropped the papers as if they were suddenly too heavy, her hands trembling visibly as she stared at the evidence of a life she hadn't known.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Liam asked, his voice tight with confusion as he surveyed the desk. He’d been looking for a misplaced textbook and stumbled upon a stack of pristine, graded papers. The sheer volume of completed work was staggering. He picked up a geometry project, the crisp edges of the paper feeling alien under his touch. A sudden dizziness washed over him, and he leaned back against the wall, gripping his temples. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming of his pulse. He felt a profound sense of disorientation, as if the very foundation of his understanding of his family had just tilted sideways.

[story 3]

I found the collection hidden behind a loose panel in the basement. It wasn't just homework; it was a chronicle of every effort, every late night spent hunched over a desk. My fingers brushed against a test paper, and a tremor ran up my arm, starting in my fingertips and spreading through my whole body. I couldn't seem to focus on the ink on the page. My mouth went dry. I stood there for a long moment, the air thick and still, watching the papers as if they were moving. A slow, unsteady shake began in my legs, and I had to consciously force myself to take a slow, measured step back, needing distance from the overwhelming reality I was confronting.
